body_txt: A familiar, spiffed up and neatly tied off version of the sensationally violent yet true story you kind of know only from a headline in the papers. Double points if the real crime sounds like something fictional . While Based on a True Story can come about at any time (a real event in ancient Greece would apply), this trope leans towards producers taking a recent hot event and repurposing it in a quick turnaround. Sometimes, murder is added to the real story to make it spicier. Other times it's an original story with original characters, but leans into recent events for more engagement factor. Often seen on Law & Order and its various spinoffs (in fact, their commercials provide the Trope Namer ), one of their core principles was to take these types of stories and audiences are already interested in the twist they will include within their format. Though it is otherwise very common in most Crime and Punishment Series , Lifetime Original Movies (who literally use the phrase in their press releases) and the like. However, the original inspiration behind the title was the stock Warner Brothers film studio's tagline "Torn from today's headlines!"; Warners used this catchphrase often beginning in the 1930s to promote the gritty realism of their "social problem" films. Often seen in political cartoons, where the entire purpose is to comment on current events. When it's done with more than one real-life story, that falls under Patched Together from the Headlines . There can sometimes be backlash to this approach, as using real names or even fabricating a similar but legally distinct version can be a detriment to actual public opinion as if done too quickly it may leave out important factors of the story that are only known MUCH later. The Evil Twin of this trope is Could This Happen to You? done poorly. If a criminal is knowingly imitating a famous murderer, that's Jack the Ripoff .
